content: 133. Statement of Bishnu Dhungel, Former FSU President, Nepal Law Campus:

I am a legal professional committed to the rule of law. I was arrested on Mangsir 10 and held for 18 days based on a 5–6 second viral video clip of me speaking about judicial corruption. My comments, which were part of my long-standing advocacy for reform based on Justice Hari Krishna Karki's 2078 report identifying 29 types of middlemen in the courts, were taken out of context and linked to the arson at the Supreme Court. I was not even present at the Supreme Court on Bhadra 24, and I do not know the individuals who set the fire. My 'attack' was a metaphorical call to fight corruption and misgovernance. My family and I have been deeply traumatized by this 'media trial' and the criminal charges against me, which are based solely on a snippet of speech without any physical evidence of my involvement in violence.
134. Statement of Tenzin Dawa Lama, Chairperson of TOB Group:

I am a tattoo artist living in Kapan. I am the founder of 'The Original Brother' (TOB), a motorcycle club. While I previously used the term 'Tibetan Original Blood' to represent my heritage, it is not a formal organization. Our club is a social service group with about 12 current members and no political manifesto. On Bhadra 23, I joined the protest after hearing about it on social media. My group of 10–12 motorcycles reached Bijulibazar around 11:40 AM. I was the person seen in viral videos wearing a jacket with 'Tibetan Original Blood' on it, but this was coincidental clothing choice. I shouted slogans like 'My Country, My Pride,' referring to a corruption-free Nepal, not any separatist agenda. During the protest, I was hit in the head by two stones while trying to tell protesters not to vandalize property. I left shortly after to seek medical treatment. I am a citizen by descent, but due to misinformation on social media, I have been evicted from my room and my shop. I am living like a refugee in my own country and request to be allowed to live a normal life as a Nepali citizen.
5.3. Statements from Physicians Involved in Treatment
135. Statement of Dr. Rupesh Yadav, National Trauma Center, Mahabouddha:

On Bhadra 23, I was on duty in the ICU. As the 'Gen-Z' protest involved students, we initially anticipated only minor security threats or light baton charges. However, after hearing media reports of gunfire, we cleared our operation theaters and wards to respond to potential casualties. Around 1:00 PM, an unexpected number of victims with serious gunshot wounds to joints, thighs, hips, and the spinal cord began arriving. We declared a health emergency and categorized patients into Red, Yellow, Green, and Black zones. By 11:00 PM, we had completed approximately seven major surgeries for life-threatening gunshot injuries. On Bhadra 24, new casualties arrived from Koteshwar and other areas, specifically those with shrapnel injuries. Some victims were bystanders hit while simply drinking coffee. Three patients died on the 23rd and one on the 24th; most were between 17 and 18 years old.
136. Statement of Dr. Binita Pradhan, K.M.C. Hospital, Sinamangal:

At KMC Hospital, we activated our Disaster Management Committee around 1:45 PM on Bhadra 23 as casualties began arriving. We converted 15 emergency beds into a 'Red Area.' Unlike typical protests involving limb injuries, these patients had gunshot wounds to the chest and head. We received 37 casualties on the 23rd, and twoSaurabh Krishna Shrestha and Subas Bohoradied that day. On the 24th, we treated 83 additional people, mostly for shrapnel and blast-related injuries. All treatment was free, and costs were submitted to the Ministry of Health. During the crisis, one of our ambulances was vandalized in Imadol, and the hospital faced threats from protesters demanding the release of a high-profile patient, former minister Mo. Aftab Alam, creating significant mental stress for the staff.
137. Statement of Dr. Ramesh Panta, Civil Hospital, New Baneshwar:

As a hub hospital, we were on alert from Bhadra 22. On Bhadra 23, casualties began arriving at 12:15 PM, quickly overwhelming our triage zones. We referred neurosurgery cases to the Trauma Center and Teaching Hospital, but ambulances faced massive blockages and vandalism. The hospital itself was affected by tear gas, and we feared arson as fires burned nearby. We treated 252 injured individuals on the 23rd, with three fatalities. On the 24th, we treated 210 people, including both protesters and police officers. To ensure safety, we removed the officers' uniforms and placed them in separate wards. Out of 462 total patients, 50 underwent surgery and 42 were referred to other hospitals.
5.4. Statements from Eyewitnesses
138. Statement of Subas Khatri:

I am a student at G.P. Koirala College and operate a snooker house in Chabahil. My friend Umesh Mahat and I were not participating in the protest on Bhadra 23. On Bhadra 24, hearing of unrest at the Gaushala Police Office, we went to observe. Around 1:00 or 2:00 PM, while we were standing opposite the station, a clash involving stones and gunfire broke out. Umesh was shot in the right chest and collapsed. We rushed him to Frontline Hospital on a scooter, but he was pronounced dead around 4:00 or 5:00 PM. I suspect the violence on the 24th was caused by outside infiltrators.
139. Statement of [Student Participant]:

I am a BBS student at Public Youth Campus. On Bhadra 23, after seeing the protest on TikTok, I went to Maitighar and followed the crowd to New Baneshwar. Protesters were throwing stones and even returning tear gas canisters thrown by the police. When the police opened fire, I heard the crowd shouting about injuries. While I was standing near the overhead bridge at the Everest Hotel, I personally saw a man, later identified as Ishwar Adhikari, get shot in the chest right in front of me. He was wearing a black helmet and jacket. I fled the scene in terror. I believe the subsequent vandalism on the 24th was the result of criminal groups infiltrating the angry crowd.
140. Statement of Kishor Ghimire, Security Guard at Thahakhabar.com, New Baneshwar:

I work as a security guard at the Thahakhabar building. On Bhadra 23, around 12:30 PM, protesters breached the restricted zone near Parliament. I personally witnessed 4–5 people fall after being shot by security forces from inside the Parliament building. Protesters searched our roof multiple times suspecting police snipers but found no one. On the 24th, a larger crowd returned. Around 1:00 PM, I saw the police surrender by raising their weapons, after which the protesters completely set the Parliament building on fire. A group of hundreds later entered our office, telling us to leave so they could burn the building because they claimed it belonged to 'Prachanda.' We turned off the power and prevented major damage, though they looted petrol from motorcycles on the premises.
141. Statement of Netra Prasad Chaudhary, Security Guard at Thahakhabar.com, New Baneshwar:

I work as a security guard for the Thahakhabar.com building in New Baneshwar through Bishwa Security Pvt. Ltd., Naxal. My regular shift is from 06:00 AM to 06:00 PM. On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was on duty. On the morning of the 23rd, I received instructions from both my agency and the Thahakhabar office to be extra vigilant as a peaceful Gen-Z student protest was scheduled. Around 09:00 AM, I saw a large number of students and youths gathering and heading toward Maitighar. By 11:30 AM, thousands were returning toward New Baneshwar in protest. Around 12:30 PM, protesters breached the restricted zone and began pelting stones aggressively while advancing toward the Parliament building. By 1:00 PM, the crowd became even more aggressive as people converged from all directions. I then personally witnessed security forces firing from inside the Parliament building; I saw 4–5 people fall after being hit. Protesters rescued the injured. As the crowd grew more violent, security teams retreated inside the building. Throughout the evening, the cycle of attacks, injuries, and security forces firing followed by retreating continued. Protesters suspected shots were being fired from our office roof because tear gas canisters (shells) fired from the Parliament side were arching through the sky and landing nearby. Groups of 8–10 protesters searched our roof four times to verify this, but no security personnel were found there. During the chaos, they broke our office locks to hide inside. I saw about 15–16 people injured by evening. Protesters repeatedly threatened that our office belonged to 'Prachanda' and would be set on fire the next day. The situation lasted until 7:30 PM. On the morning of Bhadra 24, families of the deceased and protesters gathered near Gate No. 1, chanting slogans against the Prime Minister and Home Minister. Around 8:30 AM, a truck of Nepal Army personnel arrived, conducted blank fires, and dispersed the crowd toward Thapathali, Maitighar, and Tinkune.

Subsequently, APF and Nepal Police set up barricades. After the Army left, protesters gathered again around 9:30 AM from Koteshwar, Sankhamul, Thapathali, and Old Baneshwar. Thousands breached the barricades and surrounded the Parliament building. They attacked with stones, pickaxes, iron rods, and glass bottles. Around 11:00 AM, as protesters attempted to burn the building and police fired tear gas and bullets, I saw 6–7 more people get injured. An ambulance and a motorcycle were set on fire. Protesters pelted stones at our office, and tear gas entered our building. Suspecting police were on our roof, they forced their way to the 8th floor and, finding no one, discharged four of our fire extinguishers into the air. During the confrontation, a bullet pierced our gate and entered our changing room, punching a hole through a uniform hanging on the wall. Around 2:00 PM (14:00), after Nepal Police and APF personnel surrendered by raising their weapons and exiting, I saw the protesters completely set the Parliament building on fire. The Army was not present then. Until evening, I saw people looting goods and entering the premises with motorcycles. Some protesters were walking around in police uniforms carrying captured police weapons, conducting 'victory rallies.' Around 5:45 PM, hundreds arrived, told us to leave so they could 'do their work,' and set the building on fire, claiming it belonged to 'Prachanda.'
142. Statement of Mahesh Kumar Shrestha:

I have been operating 'HARDFORD EDUCATION' in New Baneshwar for 20 years, providing Japanese language training. My office is located on the northeast corner of the New Baneshwar intersection. On Bhadra 23, around 11:00 AM, the crowd grew thick and breached the barricades at Everest Hotel. Police used water cannons and batons to control them. Protesters were demolishing the Parliament wall and entering through Gate No. 1. At approximately 2:18 PM (14:18), a bullet suddenly shattered the glass window of my office. We fled to another room. Ten minutes later, I saw that the bullet had pierced the eastern window, shattered a wooden and glass cabinet, pierced an index file, and lodged into a cemented board wall on the western side. When I called an APF DSP to ask if we could leave, he told us to stay put and stay safe. we hid on the back balcony until 6:30 PM before going home. I believe the events of the 24th were triggered by the anger over the human casualties on the 23rd.
143. Statement of [Hotel Owner/Manager - Name not specified in snippet]:

On Bhadra 24, 2082, while I was at my hotel, Gen-Z protesters at Koteshwar Chowk attacked the Koteshwar Police Circle with arson and vandalism. Several police officers fled for their lives and entered my hotel through the roof. They arrived with torn clothes, injuries, and without shoes. Since protesters were chasing them with bricks, stones, and sharp weapons, I hid the officers in various rooms and locked them from the outside for their safety. When protesters entered the hotel asking for the police, I convinced them that only foreign guests were staying there and no police were present. The protesters shattered my CCTV cameras, solar panels, and windows, causing about NPR 100,000 in damage. Once things calmed down, I gave the officers my own clothes and shoes, fed them, and sent them off. Some left at 10:00 PM that night, and others the next morning. I do not recognize the individuals who broke my property. This statement is true.
144. Statement of Laxman Shrestha:

My house is located opposite the Eyeplex Mall on the road to Ratna Rajya Lakshmi College, facing the Parliament building. I run a mobile shop on the ground floor. On Bhadra 23, the situation was normal until noon. Around 1:00 PM, a crowd of students in uniforms arrived from Old Baneshwar chanting slogans. They were chaotic and pelted stones randomly. They vandalized an ambulance in front of my shop. Once they reached Gate No. 1, they committed arson. I saw one protester who had been shot being carried away on a motorcycle for treatment. The area remained tense all day, and the roads were impassable. Things only normalized around 7:00 PM. Later, I learned via social media about the multiple deaths and injuries.
145. Statement of Suraj Parajuli:

I was at my shop during the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. Around 11:00–11:30 AM, a massive crowd including students in uniforms arrived at the Parliament building. Clashes occurred when police tried to stop them from entering. Stones were pelted at police and local houses. Protesters seemed intent on entering the building. I heard continuous gunshots. I saw one person fall near the overhead bridge at the intersection. Protesters took the injured person away in an ambulance. I went home to Minbhawan around 4:30 PM. I later learned from social media that 19 people had died and many were injured. It appears the police fired to protect a vital institution.
146. Statement of Saroj Tandon:

I have worked as a cashier at Amico Cafe for two years. On Bhadra 23, I reached work at 8:00 AM. Our cafe is located at the southwest corner of the Parliament intersection. Around 11:30 AM, I saw the streets filled with people, including students in uniforms. They were vandalizing railings, poles, and CCTV cameras. Police used water cannons and batons. Some people were using pickaxes and hammers to demolish the Parliament wall. Police announced a curfew via megaphones. Around 2:00 PM, I saw two people fall from gunshotsone in front of our cafe and one at the intersection. Protesters took them to the hospital on motorcycles. The clashes continued all day and only settled by evening.
147. Statement of [Security Guard - Name not specified in snippet]:

I work for G-Force Security at the Rastriya Banijya Bank, Baneshwar branch. On Bhadra 23, the crowd and police began clashing around 11:00 AM. Our office stayed open until 3:30 PM. Outside, there was tear gas, baton charges, and firing. The crowd shattered our north-side windows with stones. I learned of the deaths from social media. On the 24th, we were ordered to stay in civilian clothes and prioritize our own lives if things turned critical. Around 11:30 AM, protesters broke through Gate No. 3 and entered the Parliament building. They were seen carrying out tables, chairs, gas cylinders, and laptops. By 7:30 PM, protesters committed arson and broke into our office vault, looting approximately NPR 50 million in cash and 18 kg of gold and silver.
148. Statement of Advocate Parvati K.C.:

As a legal professional, I was at my Pavitra Laxmi Law Firm in Baneshwar on Bhadra 24. I learned that a curfew was imposed due to widespread arson and looting. It is my understanding that anti-state, chaotic, and violent groups infiltrated the peaceful Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, inciting violence and attacking security forces. Consequently, security personnel were forced to fire to protect the Parliament building and for self-defense. These same groups were involved in the arson, looting, and killing of security personnel. The planners and members of such groups must be identified and punished according to the law.
149. Statement of Sanunl Das:

Around 11:00 AM on Bhadra 23, the crowd at New Baneshwar became chaotic and started pelting stones at the Parliament, police, and local houses. Some were using tools to demolish the walls. Some tried to climb police posts to snatch weapons. Police announced a curfew, but the crowd ignored it. Fire broke out at the south gate. I heard about 10–15 gunshots. Around 3:30 PM, I saw two people lying on the road near the west gate as I was heading to my room in Thapathali.
150. Statement of Ram Shrestha:

I operate a mobile shop in front of the west gate of the Parliament. On Bhadra 23, protesters gathered from all sides. Security forces were initially trying to persuade them to protest peacefully. However, a group began inciting the crowd to enter the building, and some 'Bullet' motorcycles were being revved loudly to agitate the crowd. Protesters then breached barricades and pelted stones. Police used water cannons and tear gas. The protesters created a situation where police were forced to fire. On the 24th, a large number of people, including Gen-Z and others, arrived with domestic weapons, petrol bombs, and flammables. They attacked security forces, vandalized vehicles, demolished walls, and committed arson and looting inside the Parliament building and barracks.
151. Statement of Nabin Basnet:

On Bhadra 23, I was with friends at a hotel northeast of the Parliament building. I am not certain of the exact time, but hearing a commotion outside, I went to look and saw a crowd pelting stones and using catapults. Some people were demolishing the Parliament wall. The police were also firing tear gas and using batons. People wearing jackets labeled '703' were riding motorcycles unnaturally, which further incensed the crowd. Tension escalated when Members of Parliament inside attempted to leave in ambulances. During this, I saw a security official in an Armed Police Force (APF) uniform fire a shot, and someone fell in front of Gate No. 2. As soon as the person fell, people rushed them toward the hospital. The crowd had started thickening from 11:00 AM. I observed that the crowd, which mostly consisted of people from the Madhesi community, entered the Parliament building, committed arson and vandalism, and looted belongings. I saw one protester fall right in front of my house, but I didn't see who fired the shot. On the 24th, the Nepal Army was present, but arson occurred right in front of them; the Army remained silent spectators. I felt my house vibrate from the sound of gas cylinders exploding. The fire was finally extinguished by fire engines only on the 25th.
152. Statement of Biljes Karki:

Around 11:00 AM on Bhadra 23, a massive number of protesters gathered around the Parliament building and began demonstrating aggressively. Protesters of various ideologies started pelting stones indiscriminately. The police retaliated with water cannons, tear gas, and baton charges. I learned through various media outlets that a large number of protesters were killed or injured during the police action that day. The following day, the 24th, the Nepal Army, Nepal Police, and APF were deployed for Parliament security. Around 11:00 AM, protesters began breaking the Parliament wall and iron railings to enter; the Army left their positions and retreated. After entering, protesters set fire to security posts, barracks, and the Parliament building itself, as well as the APF barracks located at the nearby Department of Roads.
153. Statement of Sachin Bhandari:

On the afternoon of Bhadra 23, 2082, all shops closed after noise and chanting started at Baneshwar Chowk. At that time, many individuals in school and college uniforms were present. Protesters were pelting stones everywhere, and some were demolishing the Parliament wall and breaking the iron rods on top of it. The police used baton charges and tear gas. Shortly after, the sound of gunshots was heard. I also heard that the crowd blinded a policeman in one eye. People on motorcycles wearing jackets labeled 'TOB' and carrying black flags were provoking the crowd. On the 24th, I reached Sasto Market for regular work around 10:00 AM. By 11:00 AM, people started coming from all directions, though there wasn't much presence from the Gen-Z group that day. Around 2:30 PM, hearing that the Parliament building had been set on fire, I went there. The streets were full of people, and the building was burning fiercely. Protesters were beating and chasing the police. There was no police presence inside the building; the Army was there but acted as silent spectators. Some protesters were running away with whatever they could findflower pots, furniture, etc. Some were setting fire to parked vehicles.
154. Statement of Dalram Shrestha:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, many people in school and college uniforms were present at Baneshwar Chowk. Protesters were pelting stones and demolishing the Parliament wall. Police used baton charges and tear gas; gunshots were also heard. Individuals on motorcycles with black flags and 'TOB' jackets were inciting the crowd. The next day, Bhadra 24, people began gathering from all sides around 11:00 AM. Around 2:30 PM, they set fire to the Parliament building. There was no police presence inside, and while the Army was there, they remained silent spectators. Protesters were setting fire to vehicles and carrying away various items.
5.5. Statements from the Injured Regarding the Incident
155. Statement of Lokraj Upadhyay:

I participated in the protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. I was injured and hospitalized, so I did not participate on the 24th. I joined spontaneously on the 23rd, believing it was necessary to support national interest and good governance by controlling corruption. I reached Maitighar Mandala around 10:00 AM. Around 11:00 AM, we proceeded forward, and by 12:00 PM, I reached the south gate of the Federal Parliament in Baneshwar. When I arrived, protesters had already broken the police barricades and surrounded the building. Police were trying to disperse them. In the process, security forces used tear gas and rubber bullets, while protesters pelted stones. During this, a shot fired from the south side of the building wounded my left flank, and another shot hit slightly below my left leg, injuring me. My mobile phone, worth NPR 32,000, was also lost. Other participants helped take me to Civil Hospital. The next day, doctors operated and removed a metal bullet from my leg. I was discharged after five days and remain under follow-up. The government covered my treatment and provided NPR 20,000. Regarding the 23rd, I suspect that in the protest on the 24th by citizens angry at the state, some other group or individuals infiltrated to incite vandalism and arson. I request an investigation and legal action against the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, CDO, Police Chief, and field commanders who ordered the firing on the 23rd, as well as those involved in the destruction on the 24th.
